questions.question: Define static methods in Java interfaces and explain their primary purpose.
keyPoints: Static methods in interfaces are defined using the 'static' keyword and have a method body.
Their primary purpose is to provide utility or helper methods that are logically related to the interface.

questions.question: List the key differences between static methods and default methods in Java interfaces.
keyPoints: Static methods are used to provide utility functions, while default methods provide a default implementation for an interface method.
Static methods are invoked using the interface name, while default methods are called on an instance of a class that implements the interface.
Static methods are not inherited by implementing classes, while default methods are inherited and can be used or overridden.

questions.question: Compare and contrast static methods and default methods in Java interfaces. Discuss their primary uses, how they are invoked, and their relationship with implementing classes.
keyPoints: Primary Use: Static methods provide utility functions, while default methods provide a default implementation for an interface method to ensure backward compatibility.
Invocation: Static methods are called on the interface itself, while default methods are called on an instance of a class that implements the interface.
Inheritance: Static methods are not inherited by implementing classes, while default methods are inherited and can be used or overridden by the implementing class.
Purpose: Static methods avoid creating utility classes, while default methods allow adding new methods to an interface without breaking existing implementations.

questions.question: Explain the design rationale behind introducing static methods in Java interfaces. Discuss a scenario where using an interface static method is a superior design choice compared to placing the same method in a separate utility class.
keyPoints: The primary rationale was to allow API designers to place helper methods directly within the relevant interface, improving cohesion and reducing the need for separate utility classes.

Superior Design Scenario: A 'Validator' interface could have a static 'isEmailValid(String email)' method, which is better than a 'ValidatorUtility' class because the method is namespaced under the Validator type, making it more discoverable and logically grouped for developers.
API Cohesion: Keeping the static method inside the interface bundles related functionality together, making the API more intuitive and self-contained.
Discoverability: Developers exploring an interface in an IDE will immediately see all its static utility methods, whereas they might miss a separate utility class.
